title: Needle-felted mole
subtitle: A velvety dark grey mole with spade-like forepaws in merino
excerpt: Build a compact mole in deep grey-black merino, with the characteristic spade-shaped forepaws and dense velvety surface texture that the real animal is known for. The finished piece is about 8 cm long.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional needle-felting practice. The mole is a beloved British garden character and a well-established subject in the British needle-felting tradition.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry felting method in which barbed needles tangle and compact wool fibres without heat or water.
Wool fibre drawn into a continuous ribbon; the standard starting material for needle-felting projects.

content.text: A mole is one of the simpler mammal forms in

because the body is a cylinder, the same diameter from shoulder to hip, with a pointed nose and almost no visible tail. The distinctive velvety texture is achieved by stabbing the surface repeatedly with the size-40 needle in a final pass, creating a dense, smooth, upright pile. Use the darkest grey-black

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees onto the foam mat only. Wear a finger guard on the supporting hand.

items.name: Merino roving, very dark grey or charcoal

content.text: Roll about 15 g of dark roving into a tight sausage about 8 cm long and 2.5 cm in diameter. Stab with the size-38 needle, rotating a quarter-turn every few passes. The body should be even in diameter along its whole length, with rounded rather than squared-off ends.
Draw the front end of the body into a blunt point by stabbing at a shallow angle toward the tip from all sides. Apply a tiny wisp of pale pink roving to the very tip and stab it in.

Roll two flat oval pads of dark roving, about 1.5 cm across and 0.5 cm thick. Felt each one firm and roughly spade-shaped on the mat. Attach one to each side of the body about 2 cm behind the nose, angling them slightly outward and downward in the characteristic digging position. Stab deeply through each paw and into the body to secure.

Cover the entire body with a final thin layer of dark roving, stabbing it in with the size-40 needle using dense, even, vertical passes across the whole surface. The pile should stand slightly rather than lie flat. This is what gives the finished piece its characteristic velvet quality.
Moles have tiny, barely visible eyes. Roll two very small balls of dark brown merino, 3 mm across, and stab them near the nose. They should be subtle, barely breaking the surface of the velvet.

items.problem: The body tapers toward the tail end rather than staying cylindrical
items.cause: More needle pressure applied to the tail section than the body
items.fix: Stab the shoulder area more firmly and the tail area more lightly. Add extra roving over the narrower section and felt it in if needed.

items.problem: The surface looks felted rather than velvety
items.cause: The final-pass stabbing was not dense enough or the needle was too coarse
items.fix: Go over the entire surface again with the size-40 needle, using rapid, closely spaced vertical stabs. The pile should be visibly raised before you stop.
